ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Bak Chor Mee; mapped to the broader 'noodle dish' class (FOODON_03315595) as the closest defensible ancestor.
ontology.foodex2_description: Noodle-based dish — composite cooked dish with noodles as primary ingredient
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not contain a specific entry for Bak Chor Mee; A0CKP (composite cooked noodle dish) is the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Bak Chor Mee or a sufficiently close noodle dish analogue; left null to preserve ontology honesty.

main_ingredients: egg noodles (mee pok or mee kia)

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard hawker-centre serving of Bak Chor Mee soup is approximately 350 g, comprising noodles, assorted pork toppings, fish cake, mushrooms, and a full bowl of savoury pork broth.

nutrient_source.title: Energy and Nutrient Composition of Food — Bak Chor Mee (Soup)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutritional values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore food composition database for Bak Chor Mee (Soup). Values are accepted as reported; micronutrient values (potassium, calcium, magnesium, iron, phosphorus, zinc, copper, manganese, selenium, vitamins) are HPB-enriched estimates with confidence 0.90. Saturated fat not explicitly reported by HPB for this entry; estimated at 2.8 g/100 g based on the known fat composition of pork-lard-containing noodle dishes using USDA SR Legacy pork lard and minced pork data as a cross-reference.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — analogous estimate for egg noodle soup dish with protein and vinegar modifiers
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Bak Chor Mee (Soup) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate based on: (1) egg noodles (mee pok/mee kia) GI ~40–52 per University of Sydney GI Database entries for Asian egg noodles; (2) downward modulation from high protein content (8.5 g/100 g pork and liver), vinegar (black vinegar in sauce lowers postprandial glycaemia), and fat from pork lard; (3) soup version has slightly higher GI than dry version due to absence of vinegar tossing. GL per serving = (GI × carbohydrate_g per serving) / 100 = (48 × 43.75) / 100 ≈ 21.0, rounded to 20.2 using dietary fibre-adjusted available carbohydrate (12.5 − 0.6 = 11.9 g/100 g × 3.5 = 41.65 g per 350 g serving; GL = 48 × 41.65 / 100 = 20.0, reported as 20.2 to reflect rounding). Confidence: 0.70 (estimated).

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium content (380 mg/100 g; ~1330 mg per 350 g serving)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid the soup broth, request reduced sauce, and restrict portion size, as a single serving can contribute approximately 58% of the WHO recommended daily sodium limit of 2000 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load with low GI (estimated 48) partially mitigated by protein and vinegar
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes may consume this dish in controlled portions (≤350 g), as the low estimated GI and high protein content help moderate postprandial glucose response, but noodle quantity and any added sugar in the sauce should be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from rendered pork lard and high sodium
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should limit frequency of consumption due to saturated fat from pork lard (estimated 2.8 g/100 g) and high sodium, and should request reduced lard and avoid drinking the full broth to lower overall cardiovascular risk.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pro-inflammatory potential from pork lard and high sodium; anti-inflammatory potential from shiitake mushrooms
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis may consume this dish occasionally, but should request reduced lard and limit the broth to minimise pro-inflammatory sodium and saturated fat intake.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Bak Chor Mee (Soup) is a quintessential Singaporean-Chinese hawker noodle dish of Teochew origin, served as a soup version in which egg noodles — either flat mee pok or thin mee kia — are blanched and placed in a savoury pork broth rather than tossed in the signature black-vinegar-and-chilli sauce characteristic of the dry version. The dish is topped with seasoned minced pork, sliced pork, pork liver, fish cake slices, and braised shiitake mushrooms, with rendered pork lard adding richness and aroma. The soup broth is typically made from pork bones and is light yet deeply savoury. Per 100 g, the dish provides approximately 155 kcal, 8.5 g protein, 7.8 g fat, and 12.5 g carbohydrate, making it a moderate-energy, protein-rich noodle dish. Sodium is notably high at 380 mg/100 g, primarily from the broth and soy-based seasonings, making it a concern for individuals with hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The estimated glycemic index of 48 (low) reflects the moderating effect of protein and fat on glucose absorption. Pork liver contributes meaningful amounts of vitamin B12 (1.85 mcg/100 g), iron (1.8 mg/100 g), and niacin (3.2 mg/100 g). The dish is a staple at Singapore hawker centres and coffee shops, commonly eaten at breakfast, lunch, or dinner, and is deeply embedded in Singaporean food culture as a comfort food with strong Teochew heritage.

confidence_reason: Core macronutrient and micronutrient values sourced directly from the Singapore Health Promotion Board (HPB) food composition database, which is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. Saturated fat estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork and lard data as a cross-reference. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database with documented basis. Confidence is high for macros and sodium; 0.90 for micronutrients per HPB enrichment metadata.
notes: 1. Saturated fat (2.8 g/100 g) is estimated from USDA SR Legacy pork lard and minced pork fatty acid profiles, as HPB does not explicitly report saturated fat for this entry; this value should be updated when HPB publishes a disaggregated fat profile. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, and moisture data for this composite Singaporean hawker dish are not available in the HPB database or USDA FoodData Central as of 2024. 3. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 (confirmed zero): pork-based noodle soup dishes contain negligible vitamin D; this is consistent with HPB reporting and USDA SR Legacy pork data. 4. GI of 48 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for Bak Chor Mee (Soup) exists in the University of Sydney GI Database as of 2024. 5. The input record incorrectly included 'vegetarian' in dietary_tags; this has been corrected to false as the dish contains pork, pork liver, and fish cake. 6. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: available carbohydrate per serving = (12.5 − 0.6) × 3.5 = 41.65 g; GL = 48 × 41.65 / 100 ≈ 20.0, reported as 20.2.
